title: Chapter 16: The Burial
content: The secretary didn't look up.

That was the part that stayed with me — not the words, but the way her fingers kept sorting forms into neat alphabetical piles while she said it. Transferred. Like he was a file. Like he was a credit. Like he was nothing that required her eyes.

I stood at the counter of the Administrative Records office for what felt like a long time, waiting for her to elaborate, to offer something — a date, a destination, a reason. But she had already moved on to the next student in line, and the one after that, and my silence was just another gap in her workflow.
"Excuse me," I said. "Transferred where?"
She finally glanced up, briefly, the way you might acknowledge a persistent insect. "That information isn't available. Student requested confidentiality." She returned to her filing. "Next."
I walked out of the office and down the hall and out the front doors of the building and into the grey afternoon, and I didn't stop walking until I reached the conservatory.
Transferred.

The word was so clean. So administrative. It smelled like toner and laminate and the particular sterility of institutional language. There was no grief in it, no violence, no wound. Just a status change in a database, a line item moved from one column to another. Heathcliff Cruz: Active. Heathcliff Cruz: Transferred.

As if he'd simply been reposted. As if he'd packed a bag and caught a bus and would send a postcard when he settled in. As if the word could contain what had actually happened — the vanishing, the silence, the way a person could occupy every corner of your life and then simply not, leaving only the shape of where they'd been.

They'd erased him. Not killed him — I would have felt that, I think, the way you feel a severed limb still aching. This was something else. This was a second death, a deletion. No forwarding address. Phone disconnected. His name gone from the course rosters, his spot in the library carrel already reassigned. The physical evidence of his existence at Blackwood scrubbed clean in three business days.
I didn't cry. I want that noted. I didn't cry.
The conservatory was empty.

It was always empty in the afternoons — the morning light was better for the specimens, and the botany students migrated with the sun. I sat on the edge of the planter where we'd worked together, where he'd drawn those parallel lines in the soil with a pencil because the roots needed guidance. The lines were still there. No one had replanted. No one had smoothed the soil flat. It was a small, stupid act of preservation that no one had intended, and I hated myself for noticing it.
I pressed my thumb into the scar on my palm.

The night of the blizzard — the night everything split open — I had dug my thumbnail into the pact mark until it bled again. I hadn't meant to. Or maybe I had. The wound had been raw for days after, an angry red seam that throbbed when I gripped a pen. Now it was healing. The skin was pink and tight and shiny, the kind of new tissue that hadn't yet decided what kind of scar it wanted to be.

I held the scar and said nothing. Not to the planter, not to the soil, not to the version of him that might still linger here like a greenhouse ghost. I had no right to grieve. That was the thing I kept coming back to, the wall I built against the feeling every time it surged. I chose this. I chose the engagement. I chose the distance. I chose Julian and the Linton name and the long game and every calculated step that led to this exact moment, this exact emptiness. I chose it.
So I didn't cry.

I pressed harder into the scar, feeling the tight skin stretch, and I buried it. All of it — the ache, the absence, the way his name caught in my throat like a fishbone. I buried it deep, the way you bury something you never want to see again. But burial isn't destruction. The body is still there beneath the earth, calcifying, mineralizing, turning into something harder and denser than it was when it was alive. The grief didn't dissolve. It compressed. It became rage — the cold, hard, useful kind. The kind you could build with.
I sat there for a long time, building.
"You seem sad."

Julian's voice came from the doorway, soft and uncertain in the way it got when he was trying to be careful with me. I didn't turn around. I could see his reflection in the glass — the concerned slant of his shoulders, his hands stuffed into his pockets like he wasn't sure what to do with them.
"Let me help," he said.

I wanted to laugh. Not at him — he was earnest, painfully so, and there was nothing funny about sincerity. I wanted to laugh at the absurdity of it. Help. As if help were a thing you could offer like a coat, like a cup of tea. As if the shape of what was wrong could be reached by another person at all.
"I'm not sad," I said. "I'm focused."

It came out harder than I meant it to. Julian flinched — barely, a micro-movement around the eyes — but he didn't leave. He crossed the conservatory floor and sat down on the planter beside me, close enough that our shoulders almost touched. He looked at the parallel lines in the soil without comment. He looked at my hands, the scar I was pressing, and didn't ask.
He just reached over and took my other hand.
I let him.

I tell myself I let him because it cost nothing. His fingers were warm and steady and entirely unaware of what they were comforting me for. He didn't know about Heath — not really. He'd seen us together, maybe. Sensed something in the way I held my shoulders when Heath's name came up. But he didn't know about the chapel, or the pact, or the blood we'd smeared on each other's palms like a promise we had no language for. Julian didn't know any of that. He just knew I was sitting alone in an empty conservatory pressing a scar, and it looked like sadness to him, and he wanted to fix it.

So I let him hold my hand, and I calculated the cost. Zero. Less than zero, maybe — a fiancé comforting his bride-to-be was a data point in the plus column. It reinforced the narrative. It was strategic.
I was wrong.

The cost was not in what Julian took. The cost was in what I let go — the friction, the argument, the raw and disagreeable feeling I'd been pressing into rage. His hand on mine was anesthetic. It numbed the wound without healing it. And I let it, because numb was easier than fire, and because I'd already decided that fire was a luxury I couldn't afford.

We sat there for ten minutes, maybe more. Neither of us spoke. The conservatory hummed with its own small life — the misters clicking on and off, the soil settling. When he finally squeezed my hand and stood, I felt the absence of his warmth like an indictment.
"Come on," he said gently. "It's getting cold."
I followed him out.
The walk back to the dorm was three hundred steps. I counted.

With each step, I rebuilt the narrative. I was an architect of retrospect, and the last three months were a structure that needed load-bearing walls replaced. My engagement to Julian had always been strategy. That was the first correction. It wasn't ambivalence; it was intention. I hadn't wavered. I'd weighed the options and selected the optimal path. Every moment of closeness with Heath, every shared silence and clandestine conversation, had been calculated distance. Protection. I was protecting him by keeping him at arm's length. If I'd let him closer, the fallout would have been worse. This was better. This was cleaner.

His departure was his choice. Not my failing. He chose to leave. He chose silence. He chose the administrative erasure of "transferred" rather than the harder road of staying and fighting beside me. That was on him.
The narrative I constructed was immaculate. Clean lines, no cracks, load-bearing and true. It would hold. It would hold me.

But the truth — the truth was messier. The truth was that I knew exactly what my engagement would cost him, and I did it anyway. I sacrificed him on the altar of the long game, laid him out like an offering, and I hadn't even started to collect. The long game hadn't paid off. The Linton financial structure wasn't cracked. Robert's hold wasn't loosened. Margaret's attention wasn't diverted. I'd given up the only person who knew the full shape of the board, and I had nothing to show for it but Julian's warm hand and a scar that was healing too fast.
Two hundred steps. The dormitory rose ahead, handsome and hollow.
I walked through the front doors and did not look back.

That night, after lights-out, I reached under the mattress and found the seam in the lining. My fingers knew the path — the hidden compartment wasn't clever, just obscure, and I'd chosen it specifically because no one at Blackwood would bother looking in such an unremarkable place. The war notebook came out first. Leather-bound, slim, the kind of academic journal that wouldn't draw a second glance. Inside, my handwriting marched in tight, disciplined columns: financial structures, family trees, dependencies, leverage points. The Linton financial structure sprawled across pages twelve through nineteen — a web of holding companies and trusts and offshore accounts that would take months to fully map.
I turned to a blank page.
The heading I wrote was carefully lettered, each character deliberate:
**Cruz, Heathcliff. Status: Unknown. Asset: Irreplaceable. Cost: Incalculable.**
I stared at the word *cost* for a long time.

Not "loss." Not "absence." Not "missing." Cost. Because that's what it was. That's what he was. The arithmetic of my life had a new variable, and it was written in red ink on the ledger. Something taken. Something owed. Something that would need to be accounted for, eventually, when the books were opened and every debit matched its credit.
Cost.

I had written his name in the same book where I mapped the Linton fortune. The same book where I tracked Robert's vulnerabilities and Margaret's blind spots. The same book that held my war, my strategy, my cold and careful plans. His name belonged there — he was part of the architecture. But seeing it in my handwriting, reduced to the same clinical format as a shell company's tax ID, made something in my chest go tight and wrong.
I closed the notebook before I could write anything else.

The dried black rose was still in the compartment, pressed flat and brittle between the lining and the frame. Its petals had darkened further — from black to something closer to ash, the color of a thing that had been burned and then forgotten. I slipped the notebook back beside it, two artifacts of the same destruction, and closed the seam.

Julian had texted. The screen of my phone glowed on the nightstand: *Goodnight. I'm here if you need anything.* Sent seventeen minutes ago. I didn't respond. I let the phone dim and darken and the room swallowed the light.

I lay on my back and stared at the ceiling. The plaster was old, original to the building, with a network of hairline cracks that spread like tributaries from a central fault. I'd memorized the pattern weeks ago. Tonight it looked like a map of somewhere I hadn't been yet.

The chapel first. I thought about the chapel — the cold stone, the way our breath fogged in the stale air. The way he'd looked at me when we cut our palms, like he was seeing me for the first time and the last time simultaneously. The blood. Not metaphorical, not symbolic — real, warm, specific, smeared between our hands as we gripped each other and said the words that bound us. The pact.
I thought about the way his hand had felt in mine. Not Julian's hand — his. Different weight. Different grip. A hand that held like it was holding on, not holding together.
I pressed my thumb into the scar again, and this time I let it hurt.
*I'll find you.*
The thought came unbidden but not unwelcome. It arrived fully formed, not a wish but a declaration, carved out of the same cold rage I'd been building all day.
*When this is over, I'll find you. And if you won't come back, I'll bring the whole thing down around both of us.*
It wasn't a prayer. Prayers are petitions — humble, hopeful, surrendered to forces beyond your control. This was a threat. Threats are architecture. They have load-bearing walls. They hold.
I closed my eyes.

Three years of silence began that night. I didn't know it then — I thought it would be weeks, maybe months, a temporary absence that would resolve when the long game paid out. But the silence had already started keeping count, marking time in the space where his voice should have been. Three years, and the girl lying in this bed would become someone the girl who loved him wouldn't recognize.
But that was later. That was the cost I hadn't calculated yet.
That night, I only knew one thing: I would find him. Or I would burn everything trying.
The ceiling stared back, indifferent. The cracks in the plaster didn't lead anywhere — not yet. But I would follow them. I would follow them to the end.
